briefs.html:
Washington heads into the 4th of July holiday with a busy legislative calendar paused for recess, the Supreme Court closing a consequential term, and President Trump's financial disclosure revealing a significant new business reality: the sitting president as crypto billionaire. The NATO summit clock ticks down to next week's gathering in Ankara.

House in Recess After GOP Revolt Derails Defense Bill

The House left early for an extended July Fourth recess after a GOP rebellion over an elections overhaul championed by President Trump stalled action on the floor, including consideration of the annual must-pass defense policy bill. After a failed attempt by Speaker Mike Johnson to fulfill Trump's demand to advance the SAVE America Act, lawmakers are set to leave Washington until mid-July.

A group of 14 House Republicans joined Democrats to block a procedural rule needed to advance a slate of bills. The failed vote effectively froze the House floor for a second straight week, derailing progress on the annual National Defense Authorization Act (NDAA) and other legislation before the July 4 recess. As a compromise, Johnson had agreed to package the SAVE Act with the NDAA before sending it to the Senate — but the Senate could easily strip out the election measure before final passage.

  • The NDAA would authorize Pentagon programs and a pay raise for U.S. troops. The bill is considered must-pass legislation and has been enacted every year for more than six decades.
  • The SAVE Act, which would require proof of citizenship to register to vote in federal elections, has been a top priority for Trump. Critics argue the requirement is redundant, since federal law already bars noncitizens from voting.
  • Several of the Republicans who voted against the rule cited unrelated grievances, including demands for leadership to schedule votes on border security legislation — a sign of the broader difficulty Johnson faces in managing a fractious conference with a narrow majority.
  • Also stalled before the recess were appropriations bills for the State Department and foreign operations, leaving foreign aid funding and diplomatic programs in continued limbo heading into the summer.

Supreme Court Closes Term With Sweeping Rulings

On June 30, the Supreme Court ruled that the Constitution guarantees automatic birthright citizenship to virtually all children born in the United States. Chief Justice John Roberts wrote the 6-3 opinion, rejecting the executive order Trump issued on the first day of his second term, which sought to bar citizenship for babies born in the U.S. to parents who entered illegally or held temporary visas.

Also on June 29, the Court overturned a 91-year-old precedent that had prevented presidents from removing members of independent agencies at will — a significant expansion of presidential control over the executive branch. In a 6-3 ruling, the Court found that Trump's March 2025 firing of Federal Trade Commissioner Rebecca Kelly Slaughter without cause was lawful. However, in a separate 5-4 vote, the Court ruled that Federal Reserve Governor Lisa Cook can continue to remain in her job while her legal challenge moves forward.
